source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.9 Check your Knowledge (pp. 68-69)

questions.q: What defines a user under Linux (the seven things the chapter lists)?
options: Login name, UID, GID, command interpreter (shell), home directory, password, and command prompt
Login name, UID, GID, IP address, MAC address, password, and hostname
First name, surname, email, UID, GID, shell, and password
Login name, UID, GID and nothing else
questions.why: The chapter lists login, UID, GID, shell and home directory (in /etc/passwd), plus the encrypted password (/etc/shadow) and the prompt (/etc/profile) - seven items in all.

questions.q: What characterizes a LONG option for a command?
options: It begins with two dashes, e.g. --list
It begins with one dash, e.g. -l
It has no dash at all
questions.why: Long options begin with two dashes (--list); short options begin with a single dash (-l).

questions.q: Which of these commands actually help you search for help on a command: google, chuck --norris, info, apropos, whatis?
questions.why: info, apropos and whatis are real help tools; 'google' and 'chuck --norris' are the guide's jokes (not commands you run to read a manual).

questions.q: Which command lets you view a user's command history?
questions.why: history displays the commands entered by the user, stored in ~/.bash_history.

questions.q: Which command searches for TEXT inside a file: find or grep?
questions.why: grep searches for a string within a file; find locates files, not their contents.

questions.q: Which command searches for a FILE: find or grep?
questions.why: find searches for files and directories; grep searches text inside a file.

questions.q: Which command redirects the error stream of a command to a NEW errors.log file?
questions.why: '2>' redirects stderr, overwriting (a new file); '2>>' appends to an existing one, and the third form is redundant. A plain '>' would redirect stdout, not errors.
